sentences.base_language: After five years of studying, Isabel finally finishes her degree.
sentences.target_language: Después de cinco años estudiando, Isabel por fin termina su carrera.
sentences.grammarNote: **Por fin** (finally/at last) expresses relief after a long wait — much more emphatic than **finalmente**, which is more formal. ¡Por fin llegó el verano! Por fin terminé.

sentences.base_language: She holds her diploma in her hands and can't believe it.
sentences.target_language: Sostiene el diploma en las manos y no lo puede creer.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Sostiene** = she holds — **sostener** (to hold, to sustain) is conjugated like **tener**: **sostengo, sostienes, sostiene**.
**No lo puede creer** = she can't believe it — **lo** is the direct object pronoun (it). Word order: **no** + pronoun + modal + infinitive.
Alternatively: **no puede creerlo** — both placements of the pronoun are correct.

sentences.base_language: She calls her mother. 'Mom, I did it! I graduated!'
sentences.target_language: Llama a su mamá. —¡Mamá, lo logré! ¡Me gradué!
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lograr** = to achieve/accomplish. Lo logré = I did it / I achieved it.
lo conseguí (I managed it), lo logramos (we did it together) — all convey hard-won success.

sentences.base_language: 'My love! I'm so proud of you! You worked so hard!'
sentences.target_language: —¡Mi amor! ¡Qué orgullosa estoy de ti! ¡Te esforzaste tanto!
sentences.grammarNote:
**Esforzarse** = to work hard / to make an effort. Reflexive and o→ue stem-changing: me esfuerzo, te esfuerzas, se esfuerza.
Te esforzaste = preterite (past). Related noun: **el esfuerzo** (the effort).

sentences.base_language: Isabel thinks back on the difficult moments — the exams, the late nights, the times she wanted to quit.
sentences.target_language: Isabel recuerda los momentos difíciles: los exámenes, las noches en vela, las veces que quiso rendirse.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Recuerda** = she remembers / thinks back on — **recordar** is an o→ue stem-changing verb: **recuerdo, recuerdas, recuerda**.
**Las noches en vela** = the sleepless nights (literally *the nights on watch/awake*).
**Quiso rendirse** = she wanted to quit — **quiso** is the preterite of **querer** (irregular: quise, quisiste, quiso).
**Rendirse** = to give up, to surrender — reflexive: **me rindo** (I give up).

sentences.base_language: But she always gave her best, even when it was hard.
sentences.target_language: Pero siempre dio lo mejor de sí misma, aunque fuera difícil.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Aunque + subjunctive** (fuera) = even if it was / might be (hypothetical or softened concession): aunque fuera difícil.
**Aunque + indicative** = even though it definitely was: aunque era difícil (stated as fact).

sentences.base_language: Her friends organize a small celebration at her house.
sentences.target_language: Sus amigos organizan una pequeña celebración en su casa.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Sus amigos** = her friends — **su/sus** is the possessive adjective for él/ella/ellos/usted; context tells us it refers to Isabel.
**Una pequeña celebración** — adjective **pequeña** precedes the noun here, which is common with adjectives of size or quantity.
**En su casa** = at her house — **en** for location.

sentences.base_language: There are flowers, balloons, and a cake that says 'Congratulations, graduate!'
sentences.target_language: Hay flores, globos y una tarta que dice «¡Felicidades, graduada!»
sentences.grammarNote:
**Hay** = there is/there are — **haber** (third person impersonal): **hay** is used for both singular and plural.
**Felicidades** = Congratulations! — literally *felicities/happinesses*. Plural form from **felicidad** (happiness).
Note: **¡Enhorabuena!** is another congratulations expression, more common in Spain.

sentences.base_language: Isabel is moved. She didn't expect all this.
sentences.target_language: Isabel se emociona. No esperaba todo esto.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Emocionarse** = to be moved emotionally — not just 'to be excited.' Me emocioné can mean 'I got emotional' or 'I was moved to tears.'
Estoy emocionado/a = excited or emotionally moved — a beautiful ambiguity that context resolves.

sentences.base_language: 'Thank you all. This means a lot to me.'
sentences.target_language: —Gracias a todos. Esto significa mucho para mí.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Gracias a todos** = thank you all — **a todos** (to everyone); note the personal **a** before **todos** (people).
**Significa mucho para mí** = means a lot to me — **significar** = to mean/signify; **para mí** = to me/for me.
**Para** expresses relevance or personal impact: **esto es importante para mí** (this is important to me).

sentences.base_language: Her friend Marcos raises his glass. 'To Isabel — and to everything she's going to achieve!'
sentences.target_language: Su amigo Marcos levanta su copa. —¡Por Isabel, y por todo lo que va a lograr!
sentences.grammarNote:
**Raising a toast**: levanta la copa (raises the glass), brindamos por (we toast to).
Toast structure: **¡Por + noun/person!** — ¡Por ti!, ¡Por nosotros!, ¡Por el futuro!
